coredata.dc:title: Concentration-dependent effect of photoluminescent carbon dots on the microbial activity of the soil studied by combination methods
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wenjuan
Yao, Jun
Chai, Hankuai
Zhao, Zhibo
Zhang, Chi
Jin, Jingnan
Choi, Martin M.F.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (Cdots) have a great potential for their widespread biological applications. However, there are a few studies on the biosafety of Cdots. In this work, the biological effect of Cdots on soil microorganism was analyzed by microcalorimetry, soil enzymatic activities, and denaturing gradient gel electrophoresis (DGGE). The addition of Cdots causes a gradual increase of the maximum heat power (P peak) and the growth rate (k) at low concentration of Cdots (0.0–50.00μg/g). But there is no significant effect of Cdots on the total heat output (Q total). The urease and fluorescein diacetate esterase activities demonstrate that introduction of Cdots to soil has almost no impact on the structure and function of the soil microbial community and microbial processes. The DGGE results exhibit that the control and the Cdots-treated soils display similar patterns, indicating that Cdots have little effect on the microbial community structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biological effect
Microbial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of enzyme activity of soil sample
DNA extraction, PCR-DGGE analysis of bacterial 16S rRNA
Determination of thermokinetic parameters in the presence of Cdots
Relationship between k, P peak and concentration of Cdots
Relationship between Q total and the concentration of Cdots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Power–time curves of soil microbial activity at the presence of different concentration of Cdots.
The relationships between the thermal parameters (a) k, (b) P peak, (c) Q total and concentration of Cdots.
Urease and FDAE activity of soil treated with different doses of Cdots. The bars indicate the standard deviations.

DGGE fingerprinting profiles of 16S rDNA gene in soils treated with different concentration of Cdots after incubating 180 days. The concentration of Cdots was: (A) 0, (B) 10.00, (C) 50.00, (D) 200.0, (E) 500.0 and (F) 1000μg/g. The changed bands are numbered from 1to 4.
Thermodynamic parameters obtained from microcalorimetric studies of soil containing various concentrations of Cdots.

head.ce:title: Concentration-dependent effect of photoluminescent carbon dots on the microbial activity of the soil studied by combination methods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, and National International Cooperation Based on Environment and Energy, University of Science and Technology Beijing, 30 Xueyuan Road, Haidian District, Beijing 100083, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, and National International Cooperation Based on Environment and Energy, University of Science and Technology Beijing, 30 Xueyuan Road, Haidian District, Beijing 100083, China. Tel.: +86 10 62333305; fax: +86 10 62333305.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (Cdots) have a great potential for their widespread biological applications. However, there are a few studies on the biosafety of Cdots. In this work, the biological effect of Cdots on soil microorganism was analyzed by microcalorimetry, soil enzymatic activities, and denaturing gradient gel electrophoresis (DGGE). The addition of Cdots causes a gradual increase of the maximum heat power (P peak) and the growth rate (k) at low concentration of Cdots (0.0–50.00μg/g). But there is no significant effect of Cdots on the total heat output (Q total). The urease and fluorescein diacetate esterase activities demonstrate that introduction of Cdots to soil has almost no impact on the structure and function of the soil microbial community and microbial processes. The DGGE results exhibit that the control and the Cdots-treated soils display similar patterns, indicating that Cdots have little effect on the microbial community structure.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are one of most attractive semiconductor nanomaterials with excellent optical properties. Particularly, water-soluble and bio-functionalized QDs have played important roles in the biological, biomedical, and pharmaceutical application (Cui et al., 2001; Taton et al., 2000). However, most QDs are synthesized with heavy metal elements which possess some adverse effects on biological systems (Mei et al., 2014). In the past years, some researchers devoted lots of commitment to develop heavy-metal-free photoluminescence nanomaterials.

Carbon dots (Cdots) as a fascinating class of “zero-dimensional” carbon nanomaterials exhibit some of the same major characteristics of QDs, such as high photostability, tunable emission, and broad UV excitation (Larson et al., 2003; Wang et al., 2010; Hu et al., 2014). Besides these advantages, compared with semiconductor, Cdots possess non-blinking fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, and ease of preparation (Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009; Zhu et al., 2009). The most important criteria of Cdots are that they are free of heavy metals and more environmental friendly, inferring that they are much safer for biological applications (Tao et al., 2012). As a result, Cdots have attracted considerable attention in biological fields on account of their unique fluorescent performance. Cdots have a great potential in some novel applications through functionalizing Cdots surface with exotic functional chemistries. Despite the novel and potential applications such as drug targeting and in vivo biomedical imaging, it is necessary to thorough understanding of the effect on human health and environment. So far there are few studies on the impact of carbon nanomaterials including single-walled carbon nanotubes (Kang et al., 2008), fullerene (Tong et al., 2007), and functionalized carbon nanomaterials (Qin et al., 2014; Rodrigues et al., 2012) on microorganism or microbial community. To our knowledge, much work has not been done on assessing the dose-dependent effect of Cdots on microbial communities in soil.

Soil is an open biogeochemical system where various biochemical reactions may occur. The soil ecosystem is likely to be the ultimate recipient for nanomaterials (Nowack and Bucheli, 2007). Therefore, the soil microbial activity is considered as one of the most significant parameters of soil microorganisms and has been suggested as an indicator of soil health. Microorganisms are an indispensable part in global biogeochemical cycles and especially sensitive indicator of soil's response to the environmental changes (Riding et al., 2012). The metabolic processes in soil are related with the measurements for evaluating toxicity in ecological studies. Since Cdots are small with diameters less than 10nm, they can enter almost all areas of microorganisms in soil. And soil characteristics (soil type, pH and organic matter content) and environment factors (temperature and humidity) also affect the abundance of soil microorganisms and their activity. In this case, it will be worthwhile to exploit some accurate methods for assessing the metabolic processes of soil microorganisms in Cdots contaminated soil.

Techniques selected for investigating metabolic processes depend on the specific characteristics of system. Each research method for detecting the microbial activity has its own limitation. It is well known that microcalorimetric method is an alternative, non-destructive and effective technique to monitor metabolic processes of microorganism. As a sensitive monitor, this method continuously quantifies the entire metabolism of microbial biomass in soil, with the incubation time being the same as in actual condition (Wadsö et al., 2011). In this regard, microcalorimetry has proved to be an important technique for accurately evaluating the metabolism and microbial growth of microorganism in soil and requires only the observable difference between the treated and control incubations about the power production (Masakorala et al., 2014). In order to obtain more comprehensive and accurate results, the studies are usually combined with other methods. Soil enzymes can act as excellent indicators in the evaluation of soil quality and nutrient cycling in natural environment. The enzymes can easily respond to the external influences induced by both natural and human factors. Urease is considered as an important extracellular enzyme because of its role in the hydrolysis of urea and its application in nitrogen uptake and cycling (Chen et al., 2014). Moreover, fluorescein diacetate esterase (FDAE) is an ubiquitous enzyme present in soil microorganism. The hydrolytic activity of fluorescein diacetate is commonly used to evaluate the overall activity of soil microorganism. Additional information on the impact to the structure of microbial communities was obtained from PCR-denaturing gradient gel electrophoresis (PCR-DGGE) based on the universal bacterial biomarker for the bacterial variable V3 region following the extraction of total soil genomic DNA.

In this work, a simple one-step synthesis of water-soluble Cdots from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) as the carbon source was adopted. The size, structure and morphology of Cdots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The optical properties of Cdots including absorption and photoluminescence were also evaluated. Finally, the dose–toxicity relationship between thermodynamic parameters, enzyme activities, DGGE and doses of Cdots were applied to evaluate the toxic effect of Cdots to soil microbial activity. To our knowledge, this is the first report on applying microcalorimetry in conjunction with enzyme activity assay and PCR-DGGE method to assess the toxicity of Cdots to soil microbes.

MWCNTs (inner diameter 20nm, external diameter 40nm) were purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech Port Co. Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). Nitric acid (65% HNO3), sulfuric acid (98% H2SO4) and other reagents were obtained from Tianjin Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). All reagents of analytical grade were used without prior purification. The solutions used in this work were prepared with deionized distilled water.

Typical fluorescent Cdots sample was synthesized according to a literature method (Tao et al., 2012). In brief, 100mg of MWCNTs were blended with 15mL HNO3 and 5.0mL H2SO4 in a 50mL flask and sonicated for 30min. Then, the mixture was refluxed for 24h at 80°C. The reaction mixture was dispersed in 100mL redistilled water after cooling down to ambient temperature. Then the dark-brown solution was filtered with a 0.1μm microfilm filter. The purification was achieved by dialysis with a dialysis bag with molecular-weight cut-off of 14kDa in 2L deionized distilled water to remove the superfluous acid until the pH was almost neutral.

FT-IR spectrum was recorded on a Perkin Elmer GX-FTIR system in the range 500–4000cm−1. The size and morphology were studied on a JEOL JEM-1011 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 100kV. All fluorescence measurements were recorded on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a 150W xenon arc lamp and a quartz cuvette (1.0cm). UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-1800 absorption spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan).

The soil sample used for assessing the effect of Cdots on microorganism was collected from cropland in Baoding (Hebei Province, China) at a depth of 5–10cm. Large root fragments and particles were removed, and the soil sample was sieved to 2mm. The soil was stored in polyethylene bags at 4°C until analysis.

Soil pH was detected by using a Mettler Toledo FE20-pH pH meter (Zurich, Switzerland). The pH value was determined by placing the pH electrode in the extract solution prepared by mixing 10.0g soil and 25.0mL deionized water. The detection of organic matter (OM) was based on the reduction of weight by heating. Soil C, H and N were analyzed by a VARIO EL3 elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany). Na, K, Mg, and Ca in soil were extracted with Mehlich 3 solution by 1:10 (w/v) soil. Na and K were quantified by a FP410 flame photometer (Shanghai, China) whereas Mg and Ca were analyzed by an AA-6300C atomic absorption spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The soil characteristics were summarized as shown in Table 1 .

Microcalorimetric study was performed on a Thermometric TAM III multichannel thermal activity microcalorimeter (Järfälla, Sweden). The microcalorimeter was used to record the heat flow of microbial biological process. Measurements were performed in a sealed 4.0mL stainless steel ampoule which was cleansed and sterilized in an oven at 100°C before being used. Each soil samples (1.00g) in an ampoules was spiked with 0.20mL aqueous solution containing 5.0mg of glucose and 5.0mg of ammonium sulfate (Chen et al., 2014) and then treated with different amounts of Cdots to give a final dose of 10.00, 50.00, 100.0, 200.0, 500.0, and 1000μg/g in dry soil samples. In order to obtain a homogeneous mixture of soil and Cdots, they were mixed thoroughly by overtaxing. Simultaneously, control experiments were conducted without Cdots treatment. The experiments were performed at 28°C. The generated signal was recorded in situ by a computer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of enzyme activity of soil sample
ce:section.ce:para: Soil enzyme activities were determined by using five different treatments of soil in which the final dose of Cdots is 0.0, 10.00, 100.0, 500.0, and 1000μg/g. And for each separate subsample, they were collected after 1, 2, and 3 months of incubation. Urease and FDAE activities were determined according to the previous methods (Chen et al., 2014). The enzyme assays were carried out in triplicate for each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA extraction, PCR-DGGE analysis of bacterial 16S rRNA
ce:section.ce:para: For each sample, 1.00g soil samples with various concentrations of Cdots (0.0, 10.00, 100.0, 500.0, and 1000μg/g) were stored in sterilized tubes and incubated at 37°C. After 3 months, 0.20g soil was used to extract the total genomic DNA with the Fast DNA SPIN Kit (Aidlab Biotechnologies, Beijing, China). The extracted soil DNA was stored at −20°C for future use. For the bacteria, the forward primer was 338F (5′-ACT CCT ACG GGA GGC AGC AG-3′) with a GC-clamp (5′-CGC CCG CCG CGC GCG GCG GGC GGG GCG GGG GCA CGG G-3′) attached to the 5′ end and the reverse primer was 517R (5′-ATT ACC GCG GCT GCT GG-3′). A 16S rDNA gene fragment was amplified by PCR. The PCR program was 94°C for 5min, 30 cycles of 94°C for 30s, 60°C for 30s and 72°C for 40s and followed by an elongation 10min at 72°C. The reaction mixture contained 12.5μL of 2× Easy Tap Supermix, 0.5μL of each primer, 0.5μL of template DNA and 11μL of H2O. DGGE was carried out using 10% (w/v) acrylamide gel mixed with a 30–70% denaturant gradient. The 100% denaturant contained 7.0mol/L urea and 40% formamide. About 20μL of PCR samples were used on the denaturing gradient gel, and DGGE was run in 1× TAE (Tris-Acetate-EDTA) buffer for 10h at 60°C and 100V. The gels were stained for 20min with SYBR Green I nucleic acid gel stain (1:10,000 dilution) (Aidlab Biotechnologies Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The image of the gel was obtained using a Bio-Rad transilluminator (Segrate, Italy) under UV light.

Infrared spectroscopy is a useful technique to characterize the surface functionality of Cdots. Fig. S1 depicts the IR spectrum of Cdots. The characteristic absorption peaks at 3424 and 1601cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching vibrations and the CO stretching of carboxylate (Badruddoza et al., 2010) were observed. Cdots possessing these functional groups have excellent water-solubility and show potential for further bioconjugation (Xin et al., 2014). The morphology and particle size distribution of Cdots was visualized by TEM. Fig. S2 displays the TEM image of Cdots. Well-spherical nanoparticles are observed and apparently there is no tubular structure. The average core diameter of Cdots is about 5nm. Fig. S3 shows the absorption and fluorescence spectra of Cdots. The Cdots display strong absorption in the UV region. The shorter the wavelength is scanned, the stronger the absorption is obtained. This observation is typical for Cdots. The fluorescence emission spectra of Cdots possess multiple emission maxima when excited at various wavelengths. When the Cdots are excited at 350nm, the emission peak is obtained at 496nm. The emission spectrum is excitation-dependent. When the excitation wavelength increases, the emission spectrum is red-shifted. Again, this is also a common spectral behavior of Cdots. The fluorescence emission of the Cdots is ascribed to the poly-aromatic sp2 carbon nanostructures and various surface functional groups on the Cdots (Tao et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of thermokinetic parameters in the presence of Cdots
ce:section.ce:para: Microcalorimetry is an efficient and appropriate method which can be used to study energy metabolism of microorganisms (Yang et al., 2010). And this method has been proved to be valid as a noninvasive method to continuously monitor the activity of a microbial metabolism process in situ for prolonged time without disturbing the system. The distinguishable metabolic thermogenic curves which display the metabolism of microorganisms in the presence of various amounts of Cdots are shown in Fig. 1 . The growth curves change regularly with the increase in the concentration of Cdots. Two typical peaks are observed for all the curves which exhibit distinct lag, exponential, stationary and decline phases. For these power–time curves, they appear similar before their peak times. But there is small inflection as well as the appearance of second peaks when the soils contain Cdots. The metabolism of microorganism in soil is very complicated. The different peaks of power–time curve are from the metabolism of different microorganism, or the same microorganisms which may utilize different metabolic pathways. For the first peak, the soil microorganisms possibly adapt to aerobic metabolism. The second peak is attributing to the anaerobes adapting to anaerobic condition.

The metabolism rate constant k can be used to evaluate the soil microbial activity and is obtained from the rise part of the first peak by the linear regression of ln P
t against t:
(1)
ln
P t
= ln
P 0
+ k t
where P
t and P 0 are the heat powers of microorganism at time t and t =0, respectively. When the soil system was exposed to Cdots, the multiplying metabolism process of the microorganism is affected, resulting in the change of k. As such, the effect of Cdots on the activity of the microorganism could be calculated from the k values. By analyzing the power–time curves for soil in Fig. 1, thermodynamic and kinetic parameters relating to the microbial activity can be obtained and are summarized in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between k, P peak and concentration of Cdots
ce:section.ce:para: The metabolism rate constant k and the maximum heat power of the growth phase P peak are significant thermodynamic parameters for the metabolism, because they display the ability of microorganism to grow under specific conditions. The relationship between k, P peak and concentration of Cdots are depicted in Fig. 2a and b, respectively. Under low Cdots concentrations (0.0–50.00μg/g), the values of k and P peak increases with the increase in the dose of Cdots, indicating Cdots have a positive effect on the activity of soil microorganisms. When the concentration of Cdots is 50.00μg/g, their values reach the highest. It can be inferred that under low Cdots concentrations, it does not give deleterious effect on the growth of soil microbes. In most cases, it can be suggested to be an innate response or accommodative response. Under low concentrations of Cdots, soil microorganisms overcompensate and reduce background damage primarily, besides microorganisms repair damage. However, the values of k and P peak decrease with the increase in the dose of Cdots (100.0–1000μg/g). After adding larger concentrations of Cdots, some microorganisms in soil are inhibited or even killed to some extent while some survivors metabolize continuously. Kang et al. (2008) reported that the physical interaction of carbon-based nanomaterials with microorganism rather than oxidative stress is a direct cause of microbial death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between Q total and the concentration of Cdots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2c displays the relationship between Q total and the concentration of Cdots. Q total represents the total heat evolved during the whole process of the experiment. The change of the total heat output (Q total) is not distinct compared to the metabolism rate constant (k) and the maximum heat power of the growth phase (P peak) with the addition of Cdots. This is probably because the existence of Cdots does not alter the metabolic way of microorganisms in soil. And the toxic activity of Cdots does not immediately restrain the metabolism of soil microorganism with just a minor damage on their physiological function step by step to chronic toxicity. It is inferred that portion of the thermal effect comes from the contribution of biomass activity and the surviving microorganism, which explains why the total thermal effect is basically a constant despite the increasing concentration of Cdots. And the huge number of surviving microorganism may develop tolerance and adapt to carbon-based nanomaterial owing to the existence of bio-degrading microorganisms and the selective pressure of the xenobiotic. The thermal parameters reflect the integrated microbial activity. The surviving microorganism such as fungus and actinobacteria which grow slower than bacteria are active in the decomposition of organic matter, releasing nitrogen, carbon and ammonia (Franco-Correa et al., 2010). They may compensate for the death of sensitive microorganisms. To a certain extent, the presence of moderate organic matter stimulates the growth of some soil microorganisms.

Like the energy metabolism, reduction in enzyme activity would be expected in soil system exposed to a toxic or interfering material. These enzyme activities are excellent indicators of soil microbial activity. Fig. 3 summarizes the enzyme activity of soil treated with various doses of Cdots for up to 90 days. The results were displayed as an average of three parallel determinations and the enzyme activities were expressed as relative activities of the control. It can be observed that there is no difference in enzyme activities for various concentrations of Cdots at the beginning of incubation. There were potentially responses of soil enzyme activities to Cdots treatment. Except for the enzyme activities in soil treated with 10.00μg/g Cdots, urease and FDAE activities of other treatments were all below the control. A slight decrease of urease activity is observed with increasing the dose of Cdots when the exposure time increases. These results confirm the small inhibitory effect of Cdots on soil which is consistent with the results from microcalorimetry. Moreover, the urease activity after 30 days’ incubation resumes to the same value of the control which indicate that this variation is only temporary because the microorganisms have the recovery capability to their original metabolic function after treatment with Cdots. And the tolerant microorganisms may utilize Cdots as the carbon source at their lower concentrations. In addition, a possible explanation is that the soil microbes start to tolerate and adapt the inhibitor after 30 days which is consistent with the environmental change by transformation of metabolism. FDAE shows only a slight decrease in activity as compared to the urease activity with the increase in the concentration of Cdots. Under low Cdots dose (10.00 and 100.0μg/g), the FDAE enzyme activities show no significant change during the 30 days’ incubation. The difference in the change of activity between urease and FDAE is attributing to the fact that urease as a representative extracellular enzyme appears to be more sensitive to pollution than FDAE. FDAE can represent the overall evaluation of the activity of soil microorganism affected with Cdots to stimulate their activity.

The effect of Cdots on bacterial community of soil microorganism was studied with PCR-DGGE analysis of amplified V3 region 16S rDNA genes. This method has been proved as an appropriate approach to evaluate the change of soil microbial community structure after introducing xenobiotic compounds. DGGE profiles for PCR-amplified 16S rDNA fragment from soil after incubating 90 days with various concentrations of Cdots (0.0–1000μg/g) are shown in Fig. 4 . It is obvious that most of the bands appear consistency in soil samples from all treatments, regardless of treatment with Cdots. Only several dominant bands labeled as 1, 2, 3 and 4 in the DGGE profiles display some changes. With visual comparison, these bands are slightly lighter with the increase in the concentration of Cdots, suggesting that the introduction of Cdots changes soil structure via restraining the growth of some sensitive bacteria in soil. This finding is similar to a previous work that the effect of fullerene (C60) on soil microbial community is minimum (Tong et al., 2007). In brief, the distressed soils have little change in band pattern indicative of low diversity owing to the acquired tolerance and selection of dominant species (Sigler and Turco, 2002).

In summary, Cdots was successfully synthesized by refluxing MWCNTs with a mixture of acids. The architecture, size and the spectral properties of Cdots were characterized by FTIR, TEM, UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The relationship between k, P peak and Q total and the concentration of Cdots show that Cdots slightly affect the metabolism of microorganisms in soil. Lower concentrations of Cdots could have a stimulatory effect on the growth of soil microbes. The enzymatic activities of soil and DGGE are consistent with the results obtained from microcalorimetry which infers Cdots have less impact on the structure, function and diversity of the soil microbial community. To conclude, the toxic effect of Cdots on soil microorganism has been investigated for the first time by combining microcalorimetry, enzymatic assays and DGGE. Cdots are less antimicrobial and toxicant nanoparticle as compared with semiconductor QDs. Our work demonstrates that Cdots have great potential as nontoxic photoluminescent nanoparticles for bio-applications.
The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.
The Transparency document associated with this article can be found in the online version.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported in part by grants from the International Joint Key Project from the Chinese Ministry of Science and Technology (2010DFB23160), National Natural Science Foundation of China (41273092), Public Welfare Project of the Chinese Ministry of Environmental Protection (201409042), and Overseas, Hong Kong and Macau Young Scholars Collaborative Research Fund (41328005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.etap.2015.02.009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:
